Category: Finance & Professional Services: Banking, Insurance, Accounting, Consulting, Legal, Real Estate
Description: Impersonation threats in approvals and transactions.
Prerequisites: A basic familiarity with everyday financial processes such as approving transactions, verifying identities, or authorising payments is all that is needed to get started with this course.
Learning objectives: By the end of this course, you will be able to identify the key characteristics of deepfake impersonation threats and explain how they are used to manipulate financial authorisations and transactions. You will also be able to apply practical verification strategies to recognise suspicious activity and protect your organisation from fraudulent approvals.

lessons.Description: This lesson explores how deepfake technology is being used by fraudsters to impersonate trusted individuals in financial authorisation processes, including executives, colleagues, and clients. You will learn how convincing audio and video forgeries can trick employees into approving fraudulent transactions or releasing sensitive financial information. We will look at real-world examples of how these attacks have succeeded, the warning signs to watch out for, and the steps your organisation can take to verify identities before acting on financial instructions. By the end of this lesson, you will have a clearer understanding of the risks deepfakes pose to financial controls and feel more confident in spotting and responding to potential impersonation attempts.
lessons.content: Impersonation threats in approvals and transactions.

slides.subtitle: Understanding the technology behind modern fraud
slides.slidecontent:
• AI-generated fake audio and video that mimics real people
• Can replicate voices, faces, and mannerisms with high accuracy
• Technology has become accessible to criminals
• Creates realistic impersonations of executives, colleagues, and clients
• Represents a new category of fraud risk for financial organisations

slides.subtitle: Common attack scenarios in banking and finance
slides.slidecontent:
• Fake CEO calls requesting urgent wire transfers
• Impersonated client voices authorising account changes
• Video conferences with fake board members approving transactions
• Recorded voicemails appearing to come from trusted counterparties
• Social engineering attacks using familiar voices to bypass security

slides.subtitle: Visual and audio clues that reveal artificial content
slides.slidecontent:
• Blurry or flickering facial edges in video
• Unnatural blinking patterns or eye movements
• Slightly robotic or mechanical voice quality
• Poor lip synchronisation with spoken words
• Inconsistent lighting across the face or background

slides.subtitle: Suspicious patterns in fraudulent requests
slides.slidecontent:
• Unexpected urgency and pressure to act immediately
• Instructions to bypass normal approval procedures
• Requests from senior staff you rarely interact with directly
• Communication through unfamiliar channels or personal devices
• Inability to answer basic verification questions
• Demands for secrecy about the transaction

slides.subtitle: When timing and circumstances raise suspicion
slides.slidecontent:
• Requests arriving at unusual times (late Friday, holidays)
• Instructions to transfer funds to unfamiliar accounts
• Large amounts requested without proper documentation
• Calls immediately before or after system maintenance
• Transactions that don't match typical business patterns
• No written confirmation offered or provided

slides.subtitle: How fraudsters manipulate decision-making
slides.slidecontent:
• Authority bias makes us comply with perceived senior requests
• Time pressure prevents careful consideration
• Fear of questioning a superior creates hesitation
• Familiar voices trigger automatic trust responses
• Secrecy prevents consultation with colleagues

slides.subtitle: Multi-channel authentication methods
slides.slidecontent:
• Always verify through a separate, independent channel
• Call back using pre-established contact numbers
• Use agreed code words or security questions
• Require written confirmation for significant requests
• Implement mandatory dual authorisation for high-value transfers
• Document all verification steps taken

slides.subtitle: Creating a culture of verification
slides.slidecontent:
• Establish clear policies requiring identity verification
• Train staff to view urgency as a warning sign
• Create safe spaces to question suspicious requests
• Regularly update verification procedures
• Share examples of attempted attacks across teams
• Make verification a standard part of approval processes

scripts.script: Welcome to this course on deepfake risks in financial authorisations. Let's start by understanding exactly what deepfakes are. Simply put, deepfakes are artificially generated audio and video content created using machine learning algorithms. The technology can replicate someone's voice, facial expressions, and mannerisms with remarkable accuracy. What makes this particularly concerning for financial organisations is that this technology, once limited to research labs and Hollywood studios, has become accessible to criminals. They're now using it to create convincing impersonations of executives, colleagues, and clients to manipulate financial processes. This isn't science fiction - it's a real and growing threat that's already affecting organisations worldwide.

Now let's look at how fraudsters are actually using deepfakes to target financial processes. The most common scenario involves fake phone calls from what appears to be a CEO or senior executive, urgently requesting wire transfers to supposedly legitimate business accounts. We're also seeing cases where criminals impersonate clients, using their replicated voices to authorise account changes or approve large withdrawals. Video conference attacks are emerging too, where fake board members appear to approve significant transactions during what seems like a legitimate meeting. Sometimes it's as simple as a recorded voicemail that appears to come from a trusted counterparty, requesting payment for an invoice. These attacks exploit our natural tendency to trust familiar voices and faces, turning that trust into a vulnerability.

While deepfakes are becoming more sophisticated, they still leave technical clues that trained eyes and ears can spot. When you're on a video call, watch the edges of the person's face - you might notice slight blurriness or a flickering effect where the artificial image doesn't quite hold together. Pay attention to their eyes and blinking patterns, as these are often unnatural in deepfakes. Listen carefully to the voice quality too - even the best deepfakes can sound slightly mechanical or flat compared to natural speech. Watch for lip sync issues where the mouth movements don't perfectly match the words being spoken. And notice the lighting - it often appears inconsistent across the face or doesn't properly match the background environment.

Beyond technical imperfections, there are behavioural patterns that should raise your suspicion. Fraudsters typically create artificial urgency, pressuring you to act immediately without taking time to verify the request. They often instruct you to bypass your organisation's normal approval procedures - a legitimate senior colleague would never ask you to ignore established security measures. Be particularly cautious when receiving unexpected requests from senior staff you rarely interact with directly, as fraudsters often target these relationships where verification might be less routine. Pay attention to the communication channel too - if a CEO is suddenly calling from a personal mobile number or using an unfamiliar video platform, that's worth questioning.

The context and timing of requests can be just as revealing as their content. Fraudsters often target specific times when scrutiny might be reduced - late on a Friday afternoon, just before bank holidays, or during known busy periods. Be suspicious of instructions to transfer funds to accounts you don't recognise or that have only recently been added to your systems. Large amounts requested without proper documentation should always trigger additional verification. Timing around system maintenance is another red flag, as fraudsters may hope that technical issues will explain away any glitches in their deepfake content. Most importantly, legitimate business requests should follow predictable patterns - when something doesn't fit the usual business rhythm, investigate further.

Understanding the psychology behind these attacks helps explain why they're so effective. Fraudsters deliberately exploit authority bias - our natural tendency to comply with requests from perceived superiors without questioning them. They create time pressure specifically to prevent you from thinking clearly or consulting with colleagues. Many people hesitate to question what appears to be a senior executive, fearing it might seem disrespectful or show lack of trust. When we hear a familiar voice, our brains automatically trigger trust responses that bypass our usual caution. The demand for secrecy prevents you from seeking a second opinion, isolating you in the decision-making process. Recognising these psychological manipulations is the first step in building resistance to them.

The most effective defence against deepfake fraud is robust verification through multiple channels. Never rely solely on the initial communication method - always verify through a completely separate channel, such as calling back on a number you already have stored. Use pre-established contact numbers rather than any provided during the suspicious call. Implement agreed code words or challenge questions with regular counterparties that only the genuine person would know. For significant requests, always require written confirmation that can be verified through official channels. Establish mandatory dual authorisation for high-value transfers so no single person can be tricked into approving major transactions. Document every verification step you take - this creates an audit trail and reinforces the importance of the process.

Protecting your organisation requires building a culture where verification is standard practice, not an exception. Establish clear policies that require identity verification for all financial requests, regardless of who appears to be making them. Train your staff to view urgency itself as a warning sign rather than a reason to rush decisions. Create an environment where questioning suspicious requests is not only acceptable but expected and valued. Regularly update your verification procedures as new threats emerge and share examples of attempted attacks across your teams so everyone learns from near-misses. Most importantly, make verification a routine part of your approval processes rather than something you only do when something seems obviously wrong. Remember, the goal isn't just to catch the obvious fakes, but to create consistent habits that protect against increasingly sophisticated attacks.

Who is this for: Finance managers, accounts payable clerks, treasury staff, banking officers, loan officers, financial advisors, compliance officers, accounting staff, payment processors, finance assistants, branch managers
Team or Dept this is for: Finance, Accounting, Treasury, Banking Operations, Compliance, Risk Management
